attributes.stem:
A 10-year-old boy presents to the General Practice clinic with his parents due to concerns about a scar on his right forearm following a burn injury sustained two years ago. The parents report that the scar has become increasingly raised and red over the past six months, causing the boy discomfort during movement and affecting his confidence at school. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. His immunisations are up to date.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. The scar is 2 cm in diameter, raised, erythematous, and firm to palpation. There is mild tenderness on examination but no signs of infection or breakdown.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial approach to management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with a classic hypertrophic scar following a burn injury. Hypertrophic scars are characterised by raised, erythematous tissue that remains within the boundaries of the original wound, distinguishing them from keloids which extend beyond the original injury site. These scars often cause functional limitations, discomfort, and psychological distress, particularly in children.

Intralesional corticosteroid injections are considered first-line therapy for symptomatic hypertrophic scars. Triamcinolone acetonide (10-40 mg/mL) is typically used, injected directly into the scar tissue. This treatment reduces inflammation, decreases fibroblast proliferation and collagen synthesis, and increases collagen degradation. The result is a flatter, less symptomatic scar. For paediatric patients, topical anaesthetic cream can be applied before the procedure to minimise discomfort. Treatments are typically repeated at 4-6 week intervals until satisfactory improvement is achieved.

Topical silicone gel application is a non-invasive option that can be effective for scar management, particularly for prevention of hypertrophic scarring. However, for an established hypertrophic scar causing functional limitations, silicone gel alone is unlikely to provide sufficient improvement. It may be used as an adjunct to intralesional corticosteroids but is not the most appropriate initial treatment for this symptomatic, established hypertrophic scar.

Surgical scar excision carries a high risk of recurrence when used for hypertrophic scars, particularly in children who have higher rates of hypertrophic scarring. Surgery should be considered only after less invasive methods have failed or for scars causing severe functional impairment. In this case, with a relatively small scar, less invasive options should be tried first.

Fractional laser therapy can be effective for improving scar texture and appearance but is typically used for mature scars after initial inflammation has subsided. It is generally considered a second-line or adjunctive treatment after intralesional corticosteroids have been tried. Additionally, laser therapy often requires multiple sessions and may be more distressing for a child than corticosteroid injections.

Tissue expansion therapy is an invasive surgical technique primarily used for large scars or contractures requiring significant reconstruction. It involves placing a silicone balloon expander beneath adjacent normal skin, gradually inflating it over weeks to months, then using the expanded skin for reconstruction. This approach is excessive for a 2 cm hypertrophic scar and would not be considered until less invasive options have been exhausted.
learningPoints: Intralesional corticosteroid injections are the first-line treatment for symptomatic hypertrophic scars, reducing inflammation, thickness, and associated symptoms.
Hypertrophic scars are characterised by raised, red, and sometimes painful or pruritic tissue confined to the original wound boundaries.
Treatment of hypertrophic scars in children requires consideration of both functional and psychological impacts.
references: Mobley SR, Sjogren PP. Soft tissue trauma and scar revision. Facial Plast Surg Clin North Am. 2014 Nov;22(4):639-51. doi: 10.1016/j.fsc.2014.07.007. Epub 2014 Nov 8. PMID: 25444734. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25444734/

Garg S, Dahiya N, Gupta S. Surgical scar revision: an overview. J Cutan Aesthet Surg. 2014 Jan;7(1):3-13. doi: 10.4103/0974-2077.129959. PMID: 24761092; PMCID: PMC3996787. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3996787/
